title: Hobnobs
subtitle: Oaty biscuits with a rough, crumbly bite
excerpt: Hobnobs are oat biscuits with a rough, slightly craggy surface and a satisfying crunch. The oats dominate the texture: the biscuit crumbles rather than snaps. They take 20 minutes to make and use nothing beyond pantry staples. Good with tea and considerably better than the shop version.
sourceNotes: Oat biscuits have a long tradition in British and Scottish domestic baking, where oats were a staple grain and were used in everything from porridge to parkins to shortbreads. The commercial Hobnob was launched by McVitie's in 1985, but oat biscuits of the same basic composition appear in British cookery books from the early twentieth century. This recipe follows the standard home-baking formula: oats, flour, butter, sugar, and a small amount of bicarbonate of soda.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake in two batches. The biscuits keep well in a tin for up to a week.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 7 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 12 to 15 minutes until evenly deep gold; they firm up considerably as they cool

content.text: Hobnobs spread considerably as they bake, so leave a good gap between each one on the tray. The dough is pressed rather than rolled: roll a tablespoon of mixture between your palms into a ball, then flatten to about 1 cm thick. They will look underdone when you take them from the oven but will firm to a proper crunch as they cool. Moving them while still hot will cause them to break.

Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. In a small saucepan, melt 115 g butter with 1 tablespoon of golden syrup over a low heat until just liquid. In a large bowl, combine 100 g rolled oats, 100 g plain flour, 100 g light brown sugar, half a teaspoon of bicarbonate of soda, and a quarter teaspoon of salt. Pour in the melted butter mixture and stir until a damp, crumbly dough forms. Roll tablespoon-sized portions into balls and place on the trays with at least 5 cm between each. Flatten to about 1 cm thick with your palm. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until deep gold all over. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack.

items.problem: Biscuits are soft and do not crisp up
items.solution: They may have been taken from the oven too early. They should be a deep, even gold colour, not pale. If still soft after 10 minutes on the rack, return to a 150 degrees C oven for a further 5 minutes.

items.problem: Biscuits spread too thin and are lacy
items.solution: The butter was too hot when mixed in, or there was too much golden syrup. Cool the melted butter slightly before adding, and measure the syrup carefully.
